text:
The container ship *Meridian Grace* was already two hours late when it finally nosed into Bellingham Harbor on Tuesday morning, pushing a bow wave of gray-green water toward the municipal dock. Captain Voss radioed ahead to Lloyd's Warehouse, where three forklifts sat idle and their operators—Pete Carmichael, his nephew Derek, and a seasonal hire from Tacoma—drank coffee from thermoses.

The ship carried forty-two pallets of frozen salmon headed for Pacific Northwest Distributors in Seattle, plus eight tons of kelp meal fertilizer bound for Skagit Valley farms. Harbor master Jim Reilly watched from his office window above the Dockside Café, filling out Coast Guard inspection forms while his assistant, Maria Ochoa, updated the arrival log in their ancient Dell desktop.

By noon, the *Meridian Grace* was unloaded. Voss handed Reilly a manifest stamped in Vancouver and mentioned engine trouble off Point Roberts. "Fuel pump seized up," he said. "Had to limp in on auxiliary."

At Lloyd's, Derek stacked the last pallet of salmon into the refrigerated truck from Cascade Cold Storage. The driver, a woman named Beth Kowalski, signed the delivery receipt and pulled out onto Marine Drive, headed south on Highway 5.

By two o'clock, the ship had refueled—eight hundred gallons of diesel at $4.12 per gallon—and departed for Everett. The harbor returned to its usual quiet: three fishing boats, a Coast Guard cutter undergoing maintenance, and the steady lap of water against creosote pilings that had supported the dock since 1947.
